speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that after several weeks, you went in to be tattooed. How was that done?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some Polish and Czech girls. After we were chosen to work-- which we didn't know what kind of a work. They tattooed us. My number is 6684. Which I will never forget. And I still have it on my arm. That's my lucky number. We were tattooed. And as you see, 6,000 is quite an early stage that we were there. I got quite a nice number, small numbers, because I must have been a very nice girl. And then the girl who was tattooing me said, ah, you look like such a pretty girl. I'm gonna make nice numbers on your arms. Because usually, the girls got big numbers, all kind of funny numbers. I have very nice numbers-- [LAUGHS] --on my arm. And then-- [SIGHS] --we heard that we were going to work. And there was a very special privileged work right there in Birkenau. At the moment, we didn't know what it was. But I know one thing. All the other girls are very jealous of us that we were chosen for this wonderful work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go march out of the camp, go into another part of Birkenau, near the crematoriums. And we had to take out the clothes from the-- before the showers and sort the transports' clothes who came in. Actually, there were loads, carloads and loads of clothes from-- their belonging-- for those people who went into the crematorium, which later we found out. We had to sort those clothes. And we had to take out from this room-- which I guess you all heard of. The room they had to get undressed before they went into the shower-- the so-called 'shower,' which was a really the gassing. That's where they gassed them. So this room which was before that gas room, we had to take their clothes out, take them back to where these huge mountains of clothes and all sorts of things was, and [INAUDIBLE], and put them back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the prisoners went into this undressing room, were you there with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only the SS was in there. The boy-- no. The boys, perhaps outside. No. We went in after.

speaker: interviewer
text: After what?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the room was empty from the people. Only their belongings were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the people go after they took their clothes off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the showers, where gas was coming instead of water. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we found out once they were dead, the boys pulled them into the crematorium, which was right next door to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the shower room? Did you see the door to the shower room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw the door. But we never-- I never went in there. That-- only the boys were allowed to go in. But there was a small window, I understand. There was. I mean, I saw it. Where they got undressed, there was a small window. And the SS was standing in front of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suppose maybe there was a-- another one on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon after the prisoners went into the showers did you and the others go into the undressing room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we really didn't know because we didn't know exactly when they went in. Transports was coming-- transports, long lines. And I really don't know how long it took for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to get the clothes out before the next group came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. And then we took them back to where the magazines were. They were called magazines. And then we would come back a few times a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first went there, did you realize what was happening to the people who had left the clothing behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. There were rumors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a way, we knew it. [SIGHS] Because we saw the long lines of people coming and disappearing. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We did find out. Listen, the Czech girls were there. We saw the flames, all night, burning. And it was a terrible, terrible smell all over in the-- in the lager and in-- in our blocks and all over. It was a terrible smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the undressing room, was there any smell there from the shower room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Obviously, they must have burned them after, or they didn't let us go in. And then, after a while, the whole compound smelled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You get used to the smell. You know. Then we knew. We saw the crematoriums working and burning and with all the flames. It was going on 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you able to get close to the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the closest we got to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you could see the flames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, you could. It was a little further. It wasn't exactly by this room. So obviously, they must have taken them a little further.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were boys who pulled the people

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: --out of the shower room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't see when they were pulling them out because the boys were separate. They came from a-- a separate part of the lager. They had men's and women's. They were separated. It was called the Sonderkommando. And-- but we knew. We saw them marching. I knew-- we knew what were-- they were doing because, as much as you couldn't be in contact with them, once in a while a few words went around. And-- and they sent a couple words to us, or we sent a couple words. And this is how we knew it. I mean, after you were in the camp for a while, you knew everything was going on, especially for us because we were able to go out of the camp-- out of this particular lager, which was called the A Lager. I was in the A Lager. And we went through. We went out. On the road, we passed the B Lager and C Lager and went all the way around, which was a march about a good half an hour each day back on-- you know, each time. And we saw the people. Matter of fact, that [? C ?] Lager was the worst. They got the least amount of food, the worst clothing, and the most people went to the crematorium from there. When we marched from our work, back and forth-- by the way, the place where I worked was called Brzezinka. That was the name of it. And they gave us a white kerchief. We had to have our head covered with a white kerchief. And this is how we marched. That was how they recognized us, who we were and where we were going. Now each time we would come back at night, from the [? C ?] Lager, the people were lined up by the wires, by the electric wires, begging for food. Now we used to hide certain foods, take a-- a piece of rag or a kerchief or something, and we would throw things over, because we had food. People who came, their luggage, their packages were-- there were food in them. We used to throw things over the fence. And then the SS came and wanted to the punish us and kill us. And we had to tell-- to tell who did it. Nobody ever, ever said one word who threw the packages. And we used to do that every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- [SIGHS] Only thing was, in this is terribly sad kommando where I was working-- well, I had enough food. I had enough to drink. And perhaps that's what gave us strength-- that's what gave me strength and keep me healthy that I was able to survive, because there were many, many selections. If you know what it is, that's Mengele came and looked over the girls. And if you had the slightest pimple on or a bit skinnier, that was the end of your life. But because we had enough food that kept us healthy-- and I think that's what helped me survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the selections were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selections were terrible. You never knew if you would come out alive, ever, ever. I've been through many, many selections. There is one particular one which I will remember till the day I die because I was sure I will not survive that selection. I happened to have an infection under my left arm. And when we went into selection, it was Mengele in the middle and two SS on his side. We had to march by him. And he would lift one arm, turn you around twice, look over your body, and then sent you either left or right. When I came to Mengele, I said, that's it. And he happened to lift up my right arm, which I just couldn't believe it was true. And I was chosen to be alive. Now when we came out of the selection, you know, we used to go five girls in a row. This is how we marched always. I came to my row. And I was waiting for the girls to come out. When the selection was over, I was the only one. Nobody came out in front of me. Nobody came out in back of me. And nobody came out next to me. From the 15 girls, I was the only one who was chosen to live. I think this was the worst selection. And this was going, already, towards the end, you know. And things were getting bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was Mengele who did the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mengele? And could you describe him for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by that time, I knew him very well. Because, first of-- the first time I met him, where we got off the train, I didn't know who he was. But then he used to come around, look us over. And the girls told us, the girls who were there-- you know, the Polish and Czech girls-- that his name is Mengele. And then I went through quite a few selections by him, through his hand. He was a tall, very handsome man who would come with a-- his boots and his britches and his uniform and his dog. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was dark, dark-haired, dark-eyed, very healthy looking man-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and big. And if you would meet him somewhere on the street, you would say, ah, what a nice, handsome guy. You would never think he was such a murderer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you realized that you were the only one of the 15 that had been selected to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can't describe that feeling. But at the same time, I said, oh, my god, my mother must be watching over me. If I survived this selection, it was a miracle. But unfortunately, something was left. I got a nervous stomach. And for days, I couldn't eat. I couldn't swallow food. I was shaking inside. And as of today, it comes back once in a while when I get very excited, or when I think back, or if something happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get any medication? Was there any hospital care?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a hospital in Birkenau where people would go in when they got sick. There was wonderful doctors, Jewish doctors, women doctors, who tried to do the very best they could. They tried to give you medicine. Once I was also taken in. And the second day I was in the hospital-- I had very high fever. This woman doctor came over to me. And I said, listen, dear. You're very young. Tonight will be a selection-- actually, not even a selection. They will empty the hospital. By your bed, there is a little window. Escape the minute it gets dark. And I thanked her. And as high fever as I had, or whichever hard it was for me to climb through the window or walk, I escaped. And I sneaked into the barrack where my bed was. And then in the morning, we heard that the hospital was emptied.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them all to the crematorium, every one of them. Only the doctors survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the doctor who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying very hard to remember. I-- I see his-- her face. I can't remember her name, unfortunately. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's a terrible thing. Certain things-- names are very difficult to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, were you still being called out for the appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was every single day, twice, in the morning and at night. [SIGHS] And unfortunately, you know, Auschwitz-Birkenau was very cold already in the beginning of September. In the month of September, October, it was bitter cold. It was winter there. This was high up in Poland. Many times we were called out, we had-- and we had to take our clothes off. When something happened in the lager, like somebody wanted to escape, they called us out, and we had to take off our clothes. We had to stand naked in the bitter cold. And then, when they caught the person who tried to escape, they were hanging her in the middle of the lager, and we had to march and look at it. And while we were waiting for that, the only way we're able to keep warm-- that we got together, and one body warmed the other. This is how we got a little warmth. And after the hanging is finished, then we were able to go back to our barracks and put on our clothes. This happened twice, once when a girl tried to escape and ran to the wires-- which was fully electrified. And as soon as she touched it, the wire pulled her whole body there. And they left her there. And we had to march by and look at her. And then they said, this is what happens to everybody who tries to escape. So we knew there was no escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive any mail or packages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I'm sorry. I forgot to mention it. Right at the beginning, when we arrived, while we-- we were waiting to take and to work, one day a few SS came and took us out of our barracks, took us over to a big room. And everybody was hand out cards, postcards with pens. And we were supposed to write home, everybody at least two cards, to our relatives to tell them that we are working, and we are very well-treated and well-fed, and everything is just fine. And we had to give these cards addressed to our relatives. That was right at the beginning, because as I mentioned, I was the first transport to Auschwitz from Hungary. And this obviously had to be sent home before they-- before they brought all the other Jews. I guess this is how they wanted them to be calm-- that they received our postcards that everything is fine and we are working, which was a big lie. And we did it under the SS fires.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any relatives or friends from your hometown who came afterwards to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw their baggage. Unfortunately, I did not-- I-- I saw their baggage. I had the unfortunate situation to open it, because every suitcase, every baggage had their name on it. And I recognized it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose name was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my uncle and my aunt. My uncle, my mother's brother, whose name was Ehrman. Him and his family's package, I opened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I knew what happened to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although, I tell you, they had two daughters. Later, I found out that they were not gassed with the parents, but they were sent to [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is later on, after I left Auschwitz-Birkenau. I'll come to that when I arrived to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your father and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I guess we never found out. But I know. I saw that they burned all the people. And I never heard from my father and brother. And I never saw them after that. So obviously, you put two and two together. I saw the flames. Nobody had to tell me anything. [SIGHS] It was a sad thing. Then, of course, after the war, I tried to find something, which there was nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sorting clothing. Was the clothing from men, women, and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We sorted the clothes, the shoes separate, the clothes separate, the various packages separate. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to the clothing that you sorted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some-- I think it was sent away. They used it somewhere. I really don't know. Although, later on, when there were no more transports coming, we had to cut them up. Some of them, we had to cut them up. And we were braiding them, making braids, mostly from leather. What they used-- the Germans-- for, I don't know. I assume they sent it to the people. There were good clothes in there and coats and furs and all. I know the hair, they were processing. The skin, they were processing. I mean, there were various things.

speaker: interviewer
text: As it was getting colder, going into winter, were you still forced to stand outside without clothing for the appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. Not always without clothing. Without clothing, it was only special occasions. [LAUGHS] Normally, we were able to keep a few little rags on us. Of course, the shoes already were worn. A lot of them had paper put around their feet. See, because I was working in such a fortunate place, I had shoes and a little more clothes. And when we used to come back, many times I came in three, four dresses. And we brought lipsticks to put the red stripe on them and left them for the girls who didn't have clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will pause now to change the tape.
